title: Moroccan Chicken Tagine

overview.description: A fragrant Moroccan chicken stew gently simmered with warming spices, vegetables and dried apricots. Rich without being hot, it's a comforting family meal that showcases the balance of sweet and savoury flavours found throughout Moroccan cooking.

ingredients: Salt and freshly ground black pepper
method: Heat the olive oil in a large casserole dish over a medium-high heat. Brown the chicken on both sides, then remove and set aside.
Reduce the heat to medium and cook the onions and carrots until softened, about 8–10 minutes.
Add the garlic and all of the spices. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for another minute.
Add the chopped tomatoes, chicken stock, red pepper, chickpeas and dried apricots, stirring well.
Return the chicken to the pan, ensuring it is mostly covered by the sauce.
Cover and simmer gently for 45 minutes, or until the chicken is tender and cooked through.
Remove the lid for the final 10 minutes if you'd like the sauce to reduce slightly.
Season to taste and scatter with chopped coriander and toasted almonds before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Traditional Moroccan tagines are named after the distinctive clay pot they're cooked in. The conical lid returns moisture to the food, allowing long, gentle cooking.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How warming spices work together without creating heat, how dried fruit balances savoury dishes, and how slow simmering develops rich flavour.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: One of the anchor meals for Chicken Around the World. It's family-friendly, freezes beautifully and introduces Moroccan flavours without requiring specialist ingredients.
chapman-tips: Chicken thighs stay much juicier than breast during long cooking.
Taste before serving—a squeeze of lemon can brighten the finished dish.
Good quality paprika makes a noticeable difference.
notes.batch-cooking: Excellent for batch cooking. Freeze in family-sized portions for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Reheats beautifully and is arguably even better the following day as the spices continue to develop.
